did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses transfer learning with pre-trained Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), specifically EfficientNetB0, EfficientNetB1, and SeReNeXt-50, to perform skin lesion classification on dermoscopic images from the ISIC challenge datasets. It also proposes a multi-scale multi-CNN (MSM-CNN) fusion approach.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the investigation of image size effects on skin lesion classification using pre-trained CNNs (EfficientNetB0, EfficientNetB1, SeReNeXt-50) and the proposal of a multi-scale multi-CNN (MSM-CNN) fusion approach. It reports specific results, including a balanced multi-class accuracy of 86.2% on the ISIC 2018 dataset, which constitutes new empirical findings rather than a summary of prior literature.

title: Transfer learning using a multi-scale and multi-network ensemble for skin lesion classification
journal: Computer Methods and Programs in Biomedicine
brief_summary: This study investigates the impact of image size on skin lesion classification using transfer learning with pre-trained Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) on dermoscopic images from the ISIC challenge datasets. The authors evaluated three CNN architectures—EfficientNetB0, EfficientNetB1, and SeReNeXt-50—by resizing or cropping images to six different scales ranging from 224 × 224 to 450 × 450. They proposed and assessed a multi-scale multi-CNN (MSM-CNN) fusion approach that combines results from these networks across all scales. The results demonstrated that image cropping yields superior classification performance compared to resizing. Furthermore, the MSM-CNN ensemble strategy significantly boosted performance over single-network or single-scale models. On the ISIC 2018 test set, the proposed algorithm achieved a balanced multi-class accuracy of 86.2%, ranking as the second-best algorithm on the live leaderboard at the time of publication.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses on the classification of skin lesions using dermoscopic images from the ISIC challenge datasets. The introduction mentions malignant melanoma (MM) and non-melanoma skin cancer as context, but the experimental task is a balanced multi-class classification of skin lesions (specifically on the ISIC 2018 dataset), without listing specific histopathological subtypes as distinct output classes in the provided text.

all_task_types.details: The paper performs skin lesion classification using dermoscopic images. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (skin lesion classes) from the ISIC challenge datasets. The primary metric reported is 'balanced multi-class accuracy' (86.2%), which confirms a classification task rather than regression.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018 Skin Lesion Classification Challenge Dataset
datasets_used.description: Dermoscopic images from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) skin lesion classification challenge datasets, specifically the 2018 challenge test set.

use.training: Images were resized or cropped at six different sizes ranging from 224 × 224 to 450 × 450 for fine-tuning pre-trained CNNs (EfficientNetB0, EfficientNetB1, SeReNeXt-50).
use.testing: Used to evaluate the proposed MSM-CNN algorithm, achieving a balanced multi-class accuracy of 86.2%.
use.other: Used for pre-training adaptation via transfer learning.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported in the paper.

ISIC 2018 Skin Lesion Classification Cha.notes: The MSM-CNN algorithm achieved a balanced multi-class accuracy of 86.2%, making it the second-ranked algorithm on the live leaderboard at the time of publication. The study also found that image cropping yielded superior classification performance compared to image resizing across all explored scales (224x224 to 450x450) and that fusing results from three fine-tuned networks (EfficientNetB0, EfficientNetB1, SeReNeXt-50) across six scales boosted performance over single-network or single-scale models.

architecture_used.main_model: Multi-scale Multi-CNN (MSM-CNN) ensemble comprising EfficientNetB0, EfficientNetB1, and SeReNeXt-50
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) adapted via transfer learning
architecture_used.input_size: Six different scales ranging from 224 × 224 to 450 × 450 (via resizing or cropping)

architecture_details.ensemble_strategy: Multi-scale multi-CNN (MSM-CNN) fusion approach based on a three-level ensemble strategy
architecture_details.fusion_method: Fusing results from three fine-tuned networks trained on cropped dermoscopic images at six scales

classification_or_output_head.type: Softmax classifier (implied by multi-class accuracy metric)

preprocessing_techniques: Image resizing to six different scales ranging from 224 × 224 to 450 × 450
Image cropping to six different scales ranging from 224 × 224 to 450 × 450
conclusions: Image size has an effect on skin lesion classification performance when employing transfer learning of CNNs.
Image cropping results in better classification performance compared to image resizing across all explored scales.

A multi-scale multi-CNN (MSM-CNN) ensemble approach that fuses results from images cropped at six scales and three fine-tuned CNNs leads to the best classification performance compared to single-network or single-scale models.
The proposed MSM-CNN algorithm achieved a balanced multi-class accuracy of 86.2% on the ISIC 2018 test set, ranking as the second-best algorithm on the live leaderboard at the time of publication.
important_remarks: The study relies on the ISIC 2018 challenge test set, which is a public benchmark; performance is reported as the second-best on the live leaderboard at publication, but external validation on independent, non-challenge datasets is not provided.

The paper does not report class-wise performance metrics (e.g., sensitivity/specificity per class), only balanced multi-class accuracy (86.2%), which may mask performance disparities across specific lesion types.

No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values or confidence intervals) is reported to support the claim that the MSM-CNN ensemble significantly outperforms single-network or single-scale models.

The investigation is limited to three specific CNN architectures (EfficientNetB0, EfficientNetB1, SeReNeXt-50) and six specific image scales (224x224 to 450x450), limiting the generalizability of the 'cropping is better than resizing' conclusion to other architectures or resolutions.

The paper does not address potential data leakage risks associated with the ISIC challenge datasets, where training and test sets may have overlapping patient demographics or image acquisition protocols.

Clinical relevance is limited by the lack of discussion on deployment constraints (e.g., computational cost of multi-scale multi-network inference) and the absence of comparison with dermatologist performance.
